data.title: Ontario Confirms 25% Surcharge on Electricity Shipped to U.S.
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--The Canadian province of Ontario officially imposed Monday a 25% surcharge on all its electricity exports to the U.S., which officials say will affect 1.5 million households and businesses in Michigan, Minnesota and New York.

Ontario Premier Doug Ford said last week he would take this measure due to the continued tariff threats against Canada by the Trump administration. And with Monday's announcement, he followed through on his promise to slap the surcharge even after President Trump offered a partial reprieve on 25% tariffs for another month.

"Until the threat of tariffs is gone for good, Ontario won't back down. We'll stand strong, use every tool in our toolkit and do whatever it takes to protect Ontario," Ford said in a statement. He's scheduled to hold a press conference Monday morning.

Ontario estimates the surcharge will generate revenue of up to $280,000 a day.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: WTI Crude Approaches 6-Month Low
data.description:
WTI crude oil futures fell to the $66.5 per barrel mark on Monday, near the six-month low of $66.3 from two sessions prior amid the outlook of weaker demand and ample supply.

US President Donald Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent signaled that the US could have a period of economic hardship in the medium term due the their unleashing of trade wars with Canada, China, and Mexico, in addition to pledges of aggressive cuts in public spending.

In China, fresh data showed that both consumer and producer prices fell more than expected in February, raising concerns that consumption for goods is not being impacted by stronger credit.

This magnified the impact of the agreement by OPEC+ to proceed with oil output hikes as early as April following members' difficulty in meeting their output cut pledges.

Supply was also aided by the Trump's friendly rhetoric with Moscow, raising expectations that sanctions on Russian oil may be lifted.

data.title: Skyharbour Reports First Three Drill Holes on Fraser Lakes Deposit at South Falcon East Uranium Project
data.description:
Skyharbour Resources on Monday said partner company Terra Clean Energy completed its first three drill holes at the South Falcon East Uranium Project which hosts the Fraser Lakes B Uranium Deposit.

The company said best results included 0.3% triuranium octoxide over 12 meters of core.

The program will continue in March and is expected to consist of over 2,000 meters of drilling. Skyharbour optioned the project to Terra, and if the 75% interest is earned, Terra will fund $10.5 million of exploration expenditures and pay Skyharbour $11.1 million, of which $6.5 million can be settled for Terra shares.

"We are extremely encouraged by the results of the first three drill holes as we continue to see mineralization in each of the holes as well as what we believe to be an expansion of the deposit to the North," Terra chief executive Greg Cameron said.

Skyharbour was last seen down $0.015 to $0.32 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: ServiceNow Is Buying an AI Startup. It's Making Its Largest Deal Yet. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Stock in ServiceNow fell after the automation-software company agreed to buy Moveworks, an artificial-intelligence start-up, for $2.85 billion, signaling that the race to build autonomous AI is demanding more investment.

The acquisition of Moveworks, which offers AI assistants for company employees, is ServiceNow's biggest ever. The cash-and-stock deal is expected to close in the second half of 2025.

Moveworks was valued at $2.1 billion in a fund-raising in June 2021. It said in September last year that it had topped $100 million in annual recurring revenue.

ServiceNow provides automation tools to big businesses and has been integrating AI into its own offerings. In particular, it is racing to deploy AI agents — programs that have the ability to take simple directions and complete multistep tasks — in competition with software companies such as Microsoft and Salesforce.

"With the acquisition of Moveworks, ServiceNow will take another giant leap forward in agentic AI-powered business transformation," Amit Zavery, chief operating officer of ServiceNow, said in a statement on Monday.

ServiceNow shares were down 5.5% in morning trading, while the Nasdaq Composite had fallen 3.4%. Investors could be concerned about a shift to a more acquisition-heavy strategy under CEO Bill McDermott; he previously headed German software company SAP, where he made a series of major deals.

ServiceNow Chief Financial Officer Gina Mastantuono told Barron's that while the company could have built similar technology to Moveworks on its own, the deal would help the company to make progress faster. It will give ServiceNow 500 employees with expertise in the technology.

"It's technology plus talent. Moveworks has...built a very powerful front-end AI system for employees and it's used by some of the world's largest organizations," Mastantuono said.

ServiceNow has said its own new AI products are the fastest-growing in its history. More than 1,000 companies paying for its generative AI suite, out of a base of more than 8,400 customers worldwide. ServiceNow and Moveworks have around 250 mutual customers.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Mitsubishi UFG Sees Bank of Canada Cutting Rates Further, Contributing to Currency Underperformance
data.description:
Last week was a volatile one for the Canadian dollar (CAD or loonie), said MUFG.

hit 1.4543 on Tuesday after United States President Donald Trump's tariff hikes on imports from Canada became effective that day. The pair has since reversed the initial move higher and has fallen back towards last month's lows at closer to the 1.4200 level, noted the bank.

Despite gains in recent days, the CAD has still underperformed last week alongside the US dollar (USD) when it has been the second worst performing G10 currency. It has significantly underperformed European G10 currencies, weakening by 5.5% against Sweden's krona (SEK), by 3.4% against the euro (EUR) and by 2.8% against Norway's krone (NOK), stated MUFG.

President Trump's subsequent decisions to scale back the tariff hikes imposed on Canada until April 2 has provided some relief for the CAD and will help to dampen the negative economic fallout for Canada, pointed out the bank. The White House provided an update Thursday indicating that President Trump has decided to pause tariff hikes on imports from Canada that comply with the USMCA trade agreement on top of the previous exclusion for autos and auto parts.

A White House official stated that about 38% of Canadian imports are covered by the USMCA trade agreement. All other goods are subject to a 25% tariff apart from energy and potash which are subject to a 10% tariff. The Trump administration will decide whether to keep the tariffs in place on April 2, when it will also outline plans for "reciprocal tariffs."

The negative economic fallout from heightened trade policy uncertainty and higher tariffs will increase pressure on the BoC to lower rates further on Wednesday, added MUFG. The bank expects the BoC to deliver another smaller 25bps cut, although the return of larger 50bp cuts can't be ruled out in the coming months if the trade war escalates further and Canada's economy slows more sharply than expected.

The BoC's current estimate of the neutral policy rate is between 2.25% and 3.25%. Heightened downside risks to growth from trade disruption will offset recent positive Canadian data releases, revealing that economic growth was stronger than expected last year. Real gross domestic product expanded more strongly than expected by 2.6% in Q4 and growth in Q3 was revised significantly higher by 1.2 percentage points to 2.2%.

Growth at the start of this year was initially boosted as well by the front-running of Trump's tariffs that lifted Canada's exports. Total exports increased by 5.5% month over month in January and by 7.5% to the U.S. As a result, Canada's trade surplus with the U.S. hit a new record high of C$14.4 billion.

So far, Canada has imposed only "modest" retaliatory tariffs of 25% on C$30 billion of U.S. imports from last Tuesday and plans to step up to cover a further C$125 billion of U.S. imports within 21 days.

The bank estimates the BoC will put more weight on the disinflationary impact from weaker growth rather than the inflationary impact from retaliatory tariffs. In these circumstances, MUFG predicts that the CAD will continue to underperform in the near term.

data.title: Whitecap Resources, Veren C$15B Merger Deal Bolsters Tariff Resilience — Update
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Canadian oil-and-gas companies Whitecap Resources and Veren are combining in a $10.44 billion deal, expanding their production footprint at a time when energy companies face challenges from tariff threats and volatile commodity prices.

Whitecap Chief Executive Grant Fagerheim said the combined company would be better positioned to navigate both threats, as well as the current macroeconomic backdrop.

The deal, which the companies valued at 15 billion Canadian dollars, including debt, came after President Trump ordered sweeping tariffs on Canadian imports, including a 10% levy on all energy entering the U.S. from its northern neighbor. The tariffs were largely postponed but uncertainty still lingers over whether he will apply them in April and in what form.

Under terms of the deal, Veren shareholders will receive 1.05 common shares of Whitecap for each Veren share owned.

Merging the business and production is expected to reduce costs by about C$200 million annually and provide an immediate 26% boost to Whitecap's free cash flow generation.

Investor reaction was mixed. Whitecap Resources shares fell by more than 12% to C$8.20 in late-morning trading, while Veren's stock jumped 17% to C$8.27.

The combined company will become the largest landholder in the Alberta Montney and the second-largest landholder across unconventional Montney and Duvernay fairways with 1.5 million acres in Alberta. Together, production is expected to reach 370,000 barrels of oil equivalent a day, 63% of which are liquids.

Executives project a stronger financial position post-merger, with lower debt and an improved credit profile anticipated, providing more flexibility for shareholder returns and for long-term capital-structure improvements.

The deal is set to close before May 30.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Moody's Upgrades Viridien on Debt Reduction, Improved Maturity Profile
data.description:
Moody's on Monday upgraded Viridien's long-term corporate family rating to B2 from B3, mainly driven by the France-based geoscience company's lower debt levels and improved maturity profile.

The rating agency said the upgrade also reflects Viridien's good liquidity, its commitment to deleveraging and Moody's expectation of an improvement in the seismic data market.

The outlook on the company was maintained at stable

data.title: Oakbridge adds Higginbotham’s Ledyard as COO
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Private equity-backed Southeast U.S. insurance agency Oakbridge Insurance has hired John Ledyard as executive vice president and COO.

Ledyard will focus on optimising organisational structure, enhancing client services and supporting Oakbridge’s growth.

Oakbridge president and CEO Robbie Smith said: “As we continue to expand our business through both organic and inorganic growth, his leadership will ensure we deliver exceptional value to our clients and insurance carrier partners.”

Ledyard spent the past 10 years at Higginbotham, most recently as managing director, operations.

Alpharetta, Georgia-based Oakbridge was founded in December 2020. It has completed more than 40 acquisitions across the Southeast.

In 2023, middle market-focused Audax Private Equity bought into Oakbridge in a transaction that saw cornerstone backer Corsair Capital divest its stake in the broker.
